answer:
**No work in the Falsafa corpus uses the figure of fire (i.e., fire as a central metaphor, symbol, or philosophical principle structuring the text) in a primary or defining way.**

Fire appears frequently across several works, but always in secondary roles:
- **Literal ritual contexts** (sacrificial fires, *aupāsana*, *śrāddha* offerings, penances): Heavily featured in Hindu law texts (*smṛti*), where fire (*agni*) is a practical element of *dharma* rites rather than a figurative or symbolic motif. E.g., *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* discusses fire rituals extensively (dozens of mentions: *Aupāsana* fire for wives, *śrāddha* in secular vs. sacred fires, *homa* oblations, etc.).[^1] Similar ritual uses appear in *Manusmṛti*, *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, etc., but not investigated deeply as fire is not distinctive or central beyond standard Vedic practice.
- **Narrative/destructive imagery** (hellfire, bale-fire torment, fire-sparks in floods): In Old English Christian poetry like *Andreas* (Cynewulf), fire evokes punishment or chaos poetically but as one motif among heroic, devotional themes.[^2]
- **Poetic metaphors** (passion, love, destruction, light; e.g., candle-fire, inner fire, firefly): Recurrent in Urdu mystical poetry, especially Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (Parts 1–3), with poems like "Jugnu" (Firefly), "Shama" (Candle), and scattered uses for divine fervor or annihilation (*fana*). However, fire is one of many symbols (e.g., moth/candle, sun, stars) in collections focused on Islamic renaissance, selfhood, and spirituality—not a central figure.[^3]

The corpus (37 works: mostly *smṛti*, Old English verse, Urdu ghazals, French theory) lacks texts like Heraclitus (fire as cosmic principle) or Zoroastrian scriptures (fire as divine witness). No wiki cards, n-grams, or searches highlight fire as a structuring "figure." If seeking peripheral mentions, the *smṛti* texts treat it most pervasively.

[^1]: *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1 (e.g., [p-d38615](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-d38615), [p-f3be7c](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-f3be7c), and ~40 others on ritual fires).
[^2]: *Andreas*, Ch. 1 (e.g., [p-51b3f4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-51b3f4) on "bale-fire’s torment"; [p-e5ccfa](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-e5ccfa) on "fire-sparks").
[^3]: *Bang-e-Dara* Parts 1–3 (e.g., Part 1, Ch. 27 "Jugnu" [p-2eeb8b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/27-jugnu/translation/#p-2eeb8b); Part 1, Ch. 40 "Shama" [p-89fef5](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/40-shama/translation/#p-89fef5); ~20 scattered poetic uses).
